title: Shell Scripting Basics
description: Shell scripting is how Linux systems are automated. From CI/CD pipelines to deployment scripts to cron jobs — if you work at Enterprise, you will read and write shell scripts. This guide covers the essentials with a focus on patterns that matter in production environments.

sections.title: Script Anatomy & Shebang
sections.description: Every shell script starts with a shebang (#!/bin/bash or #!/bin/sh) and should set strict error handling. The shebang tells the kernel which interpreter to use. In Production scripts, you'll almost always see #!/bin/bash with set -euo pipefail.
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
# The standard Production header:
set -euo pipefail
# -e:  Exit on error (any command fails)
# -u:  Treat unset variables as error
# -o pipefail: Pipeline fails if any command fails (not just the last)
# -x:  Print each command before executing (for debugging)

# Making your script executable:
chmod +x script.sh
./script.sh          # Run it
bash script.sh       # Also works

# Enterprise note: Never use #!/bin/sh in production
# On Ubuntu/Debian, /bin/sh is dash (minimal POSIX)
# On RHEL/CentOS, /bin/sh is bash
# Use bash explicitly to avoid compatibility surprises

sections.title: Variables, Parameters & Quoting
sections.description: Variables, command-line arguments, and proper quoting are the foundation. Always quote your variables — unquoted variables undergo word splitting and glob expansion, causing bugs that are hard to debug. This is a Interview red flag when missed.
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Variables — no spaces around =
NAME="World"
echo "Hello, $NAME"          # Variable expansion
echo "Hello, ${NAME}!"       # Braces for clarity/concatenation

# Command-line arguments
echo "Script: $0"            # Script name
echo "First arg: $1"         # $1-$9 are positional args
echo "All args: $@"          # All args as separate words
echo "Arg count: $#"         # Number of args
shift                        # Shift $2→$1, $3→$2, etc.

# Special variables
echo "Exit code: $?"          # Exit code of last command
echo "PID: $$"               # Current script PID
echo "Last BG PID: $!"       # PID of last background process

# QUOTING — this will bite you in production:
FILE="My Document.txt"       # Filename with space
cat $FILE                     # ERROR: tries to cat 'My' and 'Document.txt'
cat "$FILE"                  # CORRECT: passes the whole filename

# Using defaults
echo "${NAME:-default}"       # Use default if unset
echo "${NAME:=default}"       # Set default AND use it

# Command substitution (two styles)
CURRENT_DIR=$(pwd)            # Modern style (preferred)
CURRENT_DIR=`pwd`             # Old style (avoid)

# Arithmetic
COUNT=$((COUNT + 1))          # $(( ... )) for integer math

sections.title: Conditionals & Control Flow
sections.description: The if/test/[ ]/[[ ]] system is unlike any other language. The test command (or [) checks conditions. The double-bracket [[ ]] is a Bash extension with more features (pattern matching, regex).
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# File tests (most common in scripts)
if [ -f "$FILE" ]; then echo "$FILE exists"; fi
if [ -d "$DIR" ]; then echo "$DIR is a directory"; fi
if [ -x "$BINARY" ]; then echo "$BINARY is executable"; fi
if [ -z "$STRING" ]; then echo "String is empty"; fi
if [ -n "$STRING" ]; then echo "String is not empty"; fi

# String comparison
if [ "$NAME" = "root" ]; then echo "Is root"; fi     # POSIX: single =
if [ "$NAME" != "admin" ]; then echo "Not admin"; fi
if [[ "$NAME" == r* ]]; then echo "Starts with r"; fi  # Pattern match (Bash)
if [[ "$HOST" =~ ^web[0-9]+ ]]; then echo "Web server"; fi  # Regex match

# Numeric comparison
if [ "$COUNT" -gt 10 ]; then echo ">10"; fi          # -gt, -lt, -ge, -le, -eq
if (( COUNT > 10 )); then echo ">10"; fi             # Modern math syntax

# File descriptors in conditionals
if [ -t 0 ]; then echo "stdin is a terminal"; fi     # Interactive session?

# elif and else
if [ "$1" = "start" ]; then
    /usr/bin/myservice start
elif [ "$1" = "stop" ]; then
    /usr/bin/myservice stop
else
    echo "Usage: $0 {start|stop}"
    exit 1
fi

# case statement — cleaner than long if-elif chains
case "$1" in
    start|restart)   action="start" ;;
    stop)            action="stop" ;;
    *)               echo "Unknown: $1"; exit 1 ;;
esac

sections.title: Loops & Arrays
sections.description: Loops iterate over files, commands, or sequences. Arrays are underused in shell scripts — they make scripts cleaner and more maintainable.
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# for loop over explicit list
for fruit in apple banana cherry; do
    echo "$fruit"
done

# for loop over command output
for file in /etc/*.conf; do
    echo "Found: $file"
done

# for loop with seq (C-style)
for ((i=0; i<10; i++)); do
    echo "Number $i"
done

# while loop — read file line by line
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "Line: $line"
done < input.txt
# IFS= preserves leading whitespace
# -r prevents backslash interpretation

# Arrays
FRUITS=("apple" "banana" "cherry")
echo "${FRUITS[0]}"          # First element
echo "${FRUITS[@]}"          # All elements
echo "${#FRUITS[@]}"         # Array length
FRUITS+=("date")             # Append

# Iterate array
for fruit in "${FRUITS[@]}"; do
    echo "$fruit"
done

# Associative arrays (Bash 4+)
declare -A COLORS
COLORS[apple]="red"
COLORS[banana]="yellow"
echo "${COLORS[apple]}"       # red

sections.title: Functions & Error Handling
sections.description: Functions organize reusable logic. Error handling patterns with trap ensure cleanup on failure — this is non-negotiable in Production scripts.
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Define a function
log() {
    local level="$1"        # local makes variable function-scoped
    local message="$2"
    echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] [$level] $message"
}

log "INFO" "Starting deployment"
log "ERROR" "Connection failed"

# Return values
is_root() {
    if [ "$EUID" -eq 0 ]; then
        return 0             # 0 = success (true)
    else
        return 1             # non-zero = failure (false)
    fi
}

if is_root; then
    echo "Running as root"
fi

# === The Production pattern: trap and cleanup ===
cleanup() {
    log "INFO" "Cleaning up..."
    rm -f /tmp/deploy.$$
    log "INFO" "Cleanup complete"
}
trap cleanup EXIT          # cleanup runs on any exit (success, error, signal)
trap 'exit 1' INT TERM     # Handle Ctrl+C gracefully

# Enterprise standard deployment wrapper pattern:
deploy() {
    local version="$1"
    log "INFO" "Deploying version $version"
    
    if ! ./preflight.sh; then
        log "ERROR" "Preflight failed"
        return 1
    fi
    
    ./deploy.sh "$version"
    log "INFO" "Deploy complete"
}

# Run it
deploy "v2.3.1" || exit 1

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Write a script that monitors a log file for ERROR patterns and sends an alert if more than 10 errors appear in 60 seconds.'

A: 'tail -f /var/log/app.log | grep --line-buffered ERROR | head -10 — but for a real solution, use a while loop with timestamp checking and a counter. In production, you'd use a proper monitoring system (Prometheus, Datadog) — this script is for bootstrap scenarios.'

Q: 'What does set -euo pipefail do, and why do you always use it in production scripts?'

A: '-e: exit on first error. -u: error on unset variables (catches typos). -o pipefail: propogate failures through pipes. Without it, 'false | true' would succeed. These three flags turn shell scripts from fragile collections of commands into reliable programs.'

Q: 'Write a script that backs up /var/lib/mysql to /backup, rotating the last 7 days.'

A: See the code block for a complete backup rotation script.
sections.codeBlock:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

BACKUP_DIR="/backup/mysql"
SOURCE="/var/lib/mysql"
RETENTION=7

mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Create timestamped backup
TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y%m%d_%H%M%S')
BACKUP_FILE="${BACKUP_DIR}/mysql_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz"

tar -czf "$BACKUP_FILE" "$SOURCE"
echo "Created: $BACKUP_FILE"

# Remove backups older than RETENTION days
find "$BACKUP_DIR" -name 'mysql_backup_*.tar.gz' -mtime +$RETENTION -delete
echo "Cleaned up backups older than $RETENTION days"

# Verify backup integrity
tar -tzf "$BACKUP_FILE" > /dev/null && echo "Backup verified successfully"

# Report to monitoring
curl -s "https://monitor.internal/report?metric=mysql.backup&value=1" > /dev/null || true

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Bash Reference Manual — Official documentation
  • Google Shell Style Guide — Enterprise standard for shell script style
  • ShellCheck — Shell script static analysis (use this!)

analogy.title: Shell Scripts as Recipes
analogy.description: A shell script is a recipe card. Variables are ingredients. Loops are "repeat until done." Conditionals are "if the oven is hot, bake; else preheat." Functions are prep steps you reuse across recipes. The shebang (#!/bin/bash) tells the kitchen which chef to use.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Script Anatomy & Shebang
practiceLabs.task: Run `set` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man set` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Variables, Parameters & Quoting
practiceLabs.task: Run `chmod` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man chmod` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Conditionals & Control Flow
practiceLabs.task: Run `bash` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man bash` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `set` | Script Anatomy & Shebang |
| `chmod` | Script Anatomy & Shebang |
| `bash` | Script Anatomy & Shebang |
| `set` | Variables, Parameters & Quoting |
| `NAME` | Variables, Parameters & Quoting |
| `echo` | Variables, Parameters & Quoting |
| `set` | Conditionals & Control Flow |
| `if` | Conditionals & Control Flow |
| `elif` | Conditionals & Control Flow |
| `set` | Loops & Arrays |
| `for` | Loops & Arrays |
| `echo` | Loops & Arrays |
| `set` | Functions & Error Handling |
| `log` | Functions & Error Handling |
| `local` | Functions & Error Handling |
| `set` | Interview Q&A |
| `BACKUP_DIR` | Interview Q&A |
| `SOURCE` | Interview Q&A |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man set`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: shell basics operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.symptom: Permission issues accessing shell basics resources
troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Script Anatomy & Shebang?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Script Anatomy & Shebang is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `set` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the shell basics directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a shell basics directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a shell basics scenario, your monitoring alerts show set failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Shell scripts are the most common type of code I see deployed in production that has  no tests
FAANG, we enforce: (1) ShellCheck runs as part of CI, (2) scripts must have error handling (set -euo pipefail), (3) no script longer than 200 lines without justification
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, shell scripting basics is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
